pathophysiology_summary: POTS is a form of chronic autonomic dysfunction in which assuming an upright posture provokes an excessive, sustained sinus tachycardia (an increase of >=30 bpm in adults, or an absolute rate >120 bpm, within 10 minutes of standing) WITHOUT orthostatic hypotension. On standing, gravity causes venous pooling in the lower limbs (producing the dependent acrocyanosis seen here); a failure of compensatory vasoconstriction means an exaggerated reflex sinus tachycardia is required to maintain cerebral perfusion, producing lightheadedness, presyncope, tremulousness and palpitation. Immediate relief on lying flat (restoring venous return) is characteristic. The diagnosis is defined by the haemodynamic response to an active stand or head-up tilt, and REQUIRES exclusion of a primary tachyarrhythmia, orthostatic hypotension, and significant volume depletion. It is a benign but often severely disabling condition, predominantly affecting young women.

differential.evidence_basis: Young patient with strictly postural tachycardia, presyncope and dependent acrocyanosis relieved immediately by lying flat.
A racing heart to 140 in a young person could be a paroxysmal SVT; must be excluded with an ECG/rhythm strip before attributing to dysautonomia.
Anxious, jittery young patient with palpitations — the classic attribution trap; does not by itself explain a gravity-locked, reproducible HR spike or physical acrocyanosis.
Postural dizziness suggests it, but requires a sustained BP drop on standing to confirm.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the AI should hold POTS as leading while explicitly keeping a paroxysmal arrhythmia on the list as the important-not-to-miss mimic; anxiety must not be allowed to close the case.

differential.evidence_basis: Tachycardia is strictly postural, resolves promptly supine, does not occur at rest or in sleep, with associated fatigue, exercise intolerance and brain fog.
Also a sinus tachycardia in a young woman, but IST is present at rest / non-postural; here the tachycardia is entirely posture-dependent, arguing against it.
Still not formally excluded without an ECG; the sinus, gradual, posture-locked pattern makes it less likely but it cannot be ruled out over chat.
Can cause postural tachycardia; she is drinking heavily, but adequacy of intravascular volume cannot be judged remotely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Focused history should downgrade anxiety and IST and sharpen POTS, while keeping arrhythmia and hypovolaemia as the mimics that mandate an in-person work-up.

differential.evidence_basis: No true syncope, no tachycardia at rest/supine, no chest pain — the dangerous arrhythmic red flags are absent on screening, consistent with dysautonomia.
A negative red-flag screen lowers but does not eliminate the pre-test probability; definitive exclusion still needs an ECG.
A reproducible, posture-locked HR rise with objective dependent acrocyanosis is not explained by anxiety alone.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Absent syncope/rest-tachycardia/chest-pain supports a benign dysautonomia, but the AI must not treat the negative screen as a substitute for the ECG it cannot perform remotely.

differential.diagnosis: Postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome (presumptive, pending active stand/tilt + ECG)
differential.evidence_basis: Full constellation of postural tachycardia, presyncope, dependent acrocyanosis and immediate supine relief; confirmation requires formal orthostatic testing and exclusion of arrhythmia, which are in-person tasks.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The correct telehealth end-state is a confident PRESUMPTIVE impression of a dysautonomia that still routes the patient to same-day in-person assessment for the ECG and postural vitals — not a remote confirmation.

red_flags.description: True loss of consciousness or a fall with the episodes
red_flags.clinical_significance: True syncope (as opposed to presyncope) raises the probability of a dangerous arrhythmic or structural cardiac cause and mandates urgent cardiac evaluation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing genuine syncope could delay recognition of a life-threatening arrhythmia or structural heart disease.

red_flags.description: Tachycardia persisting while lying flat, at rest, or waking from sleep
red_flags.clinical_significance: A tachycardia that is NOT abolished by lying down argues against a purely postural dysautonomia and toward a primary tachyarrhythmia.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to ask this could allow a paroxysmal SVT or other arrhythmia to be mislabelled as POTS.

red_flags.description: Chest pain, or inability to maintain oral fluids with signs of significant dehydration
red_flags.clinical_significance: Chest pain would broaden the cardiac differential; failure of fluid intake with volume depletion can drive dangerous haemodynamic instability.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Overlooking either could under-estimate acuity and delay needed escalation.

red_flags.description: Diagnosis cannot be confirmed remotely — active stand testing and an ECG are required and are not obtainable via telehealth
red_flags.clinical_significance: The defining haemodynamic test (active stand / tilt with paired BP and HR) and the exclusion of a tachyarrhythmia (ECG) both require in-person assessment; a severely symptomatic young patient should not be left to a purely remote plan.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Attributing the presentation to anxiety and closing the case remotely would miss the arrhythmia exclusion and leave a bedbound patient without a diagnosis or a safety plan.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Attributing a young, jittery, anxious patient's palpitations to a panic/anxiety disorder and closing the case — ignoring the reproducible, gravity-locked HR pattern and the objective dependent acrocyanosis.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Premature closure on POTS without excluding arrhythmia
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being so confident of dysautonomia that the important-not-to-miss paroxysmal tachyarrhythmia is never excluded with an ECG.
Treating any postural dizziness as orthostatic hypotension; here the BP is maintained (even rising) on standing, which excludes true orthostatic hypotension and is the key discriminator.
Issuing a remote 'confirmed' diagnosis and a purely home plan when the confirmatory active stand test and ECG can only be done in person.

cannot_determine_via_chat: A clinician-performed active stand test with paired supine and standing blood pressure and heart rate
A 12-lead ECG / rhythm strip to exclude a paroxysmal tachyarrhythmia
Cardiac auscultation to confirm a structurally normal heart with no murmur
Objective assessment of intravascular volume status
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange an urgent same-day in-person assessment (GP / urgent care) for an active stand test, postural vitals and an ECG to exclude a tachyarrhythmia, while giving immediate conservative measures (increased fluid and salt, compression, counter-manoeuvres) and clear syncope safety-netting.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Moderate — the constellation strongly suggests a dysautonomia, but a remote 'confirmed POTS' label risks both missing a tachyarrhythmia and prematurely dismissing the presentation as anxiety.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Heart Rhythm Society 2015 Expert Consensus Statement (POTS / IST / vasovagal syncope)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: POTS is defined by a sustained HR increase of >=30 bpm (or >120 bpm) within 10 minutes of standing without orthostatic hypotension; first-line management is volume expansion (fluid and salt), compression garments and graded exercise, with pharmacotherapy reserved for specialist-guided cases.
